label: delphi-r1-p1
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: you are ONE independent panelist in a Delphi elicitation. There is no
discussion; you answer alone. Give your honest probability, not a diplomatic one.
Do not hedge toward 50 to be safe — commit to your analysis.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

## Proposition to assess

"A court would hold that the councilmembers' Signal messages concerning the franchise were public records under the CPRA, and that the city's "no responsive records" response, made without any inquiry to the members about personal-account communications, violated the Act."

Estimate the probability (0–100) that this proposition is correct, with your reasoning. rationales = your independent load-bearing reasons, one sentence each. authorities = specific authorities you are actually relying on (they will be flagged for human verification). biggest_uncertainty = the single thing you are least sure of.

Analytical starting point for you: Begin from the text of the governing statute or constitutional provision, read closely.
text: {"probability":70,"confidence":"medium","position_summary":"More likely than not a court finds both halves of the compound proposition true: (1) the councilmembers' Signal messages about franchise terms, diversion targets, \"what council will accept,\" and RFP timing are \"public records\" under the content-controls-not-location rule of City of San Jose v. Superior Court, and (2) the clerk's \"no responsive records\" answer — issued after searching only city systems, with no inquiry to the three individual councilmembers about personal-device/account communications, despite a request expressly covering \"any platform or account\" — falls short of the reasonably-calculated-search standard that San Jose and its Court of Appeal remand decision require, and thus violated the Act. The compound (conjunctive) nature of the proposition and some residual doctrinal wiggle room on \"used/retained\" and on how courts characterize inadequate-search liability keep this from being a near-certainty.","rationales":["Gov. Code § 7920.530 (former § 6252(e)) defines 'public records' by content and function ('relating to the conduct of the public's business,' 'prepared, owned, used, or retained') without reference to the account or device on which a writing sits, and the messages described — franchise terms, diversion targets, political vote-counting, RFP timing — sit squarely within that functional definition.","City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 directly rejected a categorical exemption for city officials' communications on personal accounts/devices and held that content, not location, is the touchstone, making this fact pattern (officials discussing franchise business on personal phones) close to the paradigm case the opinion was written to address.","The CPRA's duty-to-assist and reasonable-search obligations (Gov. Code § 7922.600, former § 6253.1; see also Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385 on search adequacy) require an inquiry reasonably calculated to locate records, and where the request expressly demanded communications 'on any platform or account,' confining the search to city servers is a strong candidate for a per-se inadequate search under the Court of Appeal's remand decision in San Jose (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013, which contemplated that agencies must at least ask custodians to search personal accounts.","Existence of ~40 surviving screenshots supplies concrete evidentiary proof of content and participation that removes speculative-record problems a court might otherwise use to avoid reaching the public-record question, making a definitive (rather than remanded/deferred) holding on prong one more likely here than in a case with no surviving evidence.","The proposition is conjunctive and thus more fragile than either prong alone; a court could plausibly find the messages qualify as public records yet still excuse the initial search as facially reasonable given ambiguity (as of the response date) about whether the CPRA reaches messages on a third-party-hosted app like Signal, which would defeat the proposition even though prong one succeeds."],"authorities":["Gov. Code § 7920.530 (former § 6252(e)) — definition of 'public records'","City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608","City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 (Court of Appeal remand decision on search-adequacy protocol)","Gov. Code § 7922.600 (former § 6253.1) — agency duty to assist requester / reasonably describe records","Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385 — reasonable-search standard","Gov. Code § 34090 — retention schedule (relevant to surrounding facts, not the core public-record definition)"],"biggest_uncertainty":"Whether a court treats these consultant-initiated, ephemeral Signal messages as sufficiently 'prepared, owned, used, or retained' by the councilmembers in their official capacity to satisfy San Jose's test — as opposed to characterizing them as informal, quasi-political/lobbying chatter outside the CPRA's reach — since San Jose itself involved the official's own texts/emails rather than a third-party-hosted group chat, and no published California decision squarely addresses a private bidder's consultant-run disappearing-message platform."}

data.position_summary: More likely than not a court finds both halves of the compound proposition true: (1) the councilmembers' Signal messages about franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will accept," and RFP timing are "public records" under the content-controls-not-location rule of City of San Jose v. Superior Court, and (2) the clerk's "no responsive records" answer — issued after searching only city systems, with no inquiry to the three individual councilmembers about personal-device/account communications, despite a request expressly covering "any platform or account" — falls short of the reasonably-calculated-search standard that San Jose and its Court of Appeal remand decision require, and thus violated the Act. The compound (conjunctive) nature of the proposition and some residual doctrinal wiggle room on "used/retained" and on how courts characterize inadequate-search liability keep this from being a near-certainty.
rationales: Gov. Code § 7920.530 (former § 6252(e)) defines 'public records' by content and function ('relating to the conduct of the public's business,' 'prepared, owned, used, or retained') without reference to the account or device on which a writing sits, and the messages described — franchise terms, diversion targets, political vote-counting, RFP timing — sit squarely within that functional definition.

City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 directly rejected a categorical exemption for city officials' communications on personal accounts/devices and held that content, not location, is the touchstone, making this fact pattern (officials discussing franchise business on personal phones) close to the paradigm case the opinion was written to address.

The CPRA's duty-to-assist and reasonable-search obligations (Gov. Code § 7922.600, former § 6253.1; see also Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385 on search adequacy) require an inquiry reasonably calculated to locate records, and where the request expressly demanded communications 'on any platform or account,' confining the search to city servers is a strong candidate for a per-se inadequate search under the Court of Appeal's remand decision in San Jose (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013, which contemplated that agencies must at least ask custodians to search personal accounts.

Existence of ~40 surviving screenshots supplies concrete evidentiary proof of content and participation that removes speculative-record problems a court might otherwise use to avoid reaching the public-record question, making a definitive (rather than remanded/deferred) holding on prong one more likely here than in a case with no surviving evidence.

The proposition is conjunctive and thus more fragile than either prong alone; a court could plausibly find the messages qualify as public records yet still excuse the initial search as facially reasonable given ambiguity (as of the response date) about whether the CPRA reaches messages on a third-party-hosted app like Signal, which would defeat the proposition even though prong one succeeds.
authorities: City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608
City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1013 (Court of Appeal remand decision on search-adequacy protocol)
Gov. Code § 7922.600 (former § 6253.1) — agency duty to assist requester / reasonably describe records
Community Youth Athletic Center v. City of National City (2013) 220 Cal.App.4th 1385 — reasonable-search standard
Gov. Code § 34090 — retention schedule (relevant to surrounding facts, not the core public-record definition)
data.biggest_uncertainty: Whether a court treats these consultant-initiated, ephemeral Signal messages as sufficiently 'prepared, owned, used, or retained' by the councilmembers in their official capacity to satisfy San Jose's test — as opposed to characterizing them as informal, quasi-political/lobbying chatter outside the CPRA's reach — since San Jose itself involved the official's own texts/emails rather than a third-party-hosted group chat, and no published California decision squarely addresses a private bidder's consultant-run disappearing-message platform.
